title: How to Create a Financial Vision That Excites You
body:
Your Financial Vision is Probably Boring. Let's Fix That.

Here's the thing. Your financial vision, if you even have one, probably sucks. It's not your fault. We've been fed this narrative about money that's all about scarcity, struggle, and deprivation. It's about budgeting until your eyeballs bleed, saving for a future that feels abstract, and constantly worrying you don't have enough. Sound familiar?

You're told to "set financial goals." But what are those goals, really? Pay off debt? Save for retirement? Buy a house? These are milestones, sure, but they're often presented in such a dry, lifeless way that they fail to ignite anything within you. They're like a grocery list without the promise of a delicious meal. No wonder you struggle to stick to a budget, or feel perpetually behind. Your soul isn't invested. Your heart isn't in it. It's just numbers on a spreadsheet, and who gets excited about that?

We're talking about a financial vision that actually excites you. A vision that pulls you forward, not one you have to constantly push yourself towards. A vision so compelling, so deeply aligned with your truest desires, that it makes the "hard work" feel less like work and more like an adventure. Because here's the truth: Money is just energy with a story attached to it. And if your story is boring, your energy will be too.

Why Your Current "Goals" Don't Work

Honestly, most financial advice is built on fear. Fear of not having enough. Fear of being poor. Fear of missing out. This fear-based approach might get you to take some action, but it's unsustainable. It drains you. It makes money feel like a burden, a constant source of anxiety. You end up chasing a number, thinking that once you hit it, all your problems will magically disappear. But they don't, do they? You just find a new number to chase, a new fear to contend with.

Brad Klontz, a brilliant financial psychologist, talks about "money scripts" - unconscious beliefs we hold about money that dictate our behavior. Many of these scripts are negative: "Money is evil," "I don't deserve money," "Rich people are greedy." These scripts run deep, often formed in childhood, and they sabotage our efforts before we even begin. How can you create an exciting financial vision when you're operating under a subconscious belief that money is fundamentally bad or unattainable?

You can't. It's like trying to build a mansion on quicksand. You need to address the foundation first. This isn't about budgeting better or finding a higher-paying job (though those things can certainly help). This is about excavating the stories you tell yourself about money, about what's possible for you, about what you deserve. It's about connecting money to your deepest values, your wildest dreams, your most authentic self. You feel that?

The Power of a Vision That Ignites You

Imagine waking up every day not dreading your finances, but feeling a spark of excitement. Imagine making financial decisions not out of obligation, but out of a deep sense of purpose and joy. This isn't some woo-woo fantasy. This is entirely possible, and it starts with crafting a financial vision that truly speaks to your soul. This is about asking yourself, "What if your relationship with money is the most honest relationship you have?" Because it often reveals where you're holding back, where you're playing small, where you're not fully trusting yourself.

George Kinder, the father of the Life Planning movement, understood this implicitly. He didn't just ask clients about their assets; he asked them about their lives. He'd ask questions like, "If you had five to ten years left to live, and you had all the money you needed, how would you live?" Or, "If you only had 24 hours left, what would you regret not doing?" These aren't financial questions in the traditional sense, but they're deeply financial in their implications. They force you to confront what truly matters, what you'd regret if you never pursued it. And that's where your real financial vision lies.

Your financial vision isn't just about a number in your bank account. It's about the life that number allows you to live. It's about the experiences you'll have, the people you'll impact, the freedom you'll feel. It's about aligning your money with your deepest values, and using it as a tool to create the life you were meant to live. Wealth isn't what you accumulate. It's what you stop fearing. And when you stop fearing, you start living.

Unearthing Your True Desires

So, how do you find this exciting vision? You start by going inward. You start by getting brutally honest with yourself. This isn't about what society tells you you should want, or what your parents wanted for you. This is about YOU. What truly lights you up? What brings you joy? What makes you feel alive?

Grab a journal. Seriously, right now. Put away your phone. Find a quiet space. And just write. Don't edit. Don't censor. Just let it flow. Here are some prompts to get you started:

  • If money were no object, what would your ideal day look like? From the moment you wake up to the moment you go to sleep. Be specific. Where are you? Who are you with? What are you doing? What are you feeling?
  • What are the experiences you crave? Travel? Learning a new skill? Starting a passion project? Giving back to your community?
  • What does "freedom" mean to you, truly? Is it time freedom? Geographic freedom? Creative freedom? Financial freedom from worry?
  • What impact do you want to have on the world, big or small? How can your resources support that?
  • What are your deepest fears about money, and what would it feel like to release them?
  • What does "enough" look like for you, not just financially, but in terms of well-being, relationships, and purpose?

Don't be afraid to dream big. Don't be afraid to be "unrealistic." This isn't about crafting a budget yet. This is about painting a vivid picture of the life you want to create. This is about connecting with your inner wisdom, the part of you that knows what you truly desire. Geneen Roth, in her book Sacred Success (paid link), explores this connection between our inner lives and our outer manifestations. She helps you see that your relationship with money is a mirror of your relationship with yourself. You can't expect your financial life to be vibrant if your inner life is neglected.

This deep dive isn't always comfortable. You might uncover some uncomfortable truths about where you've been compromising, where you've been living a life that isn't truly yours. That's okay. That's part of the healing. As Sam Harris often reminds us, self-knowledge, even when painful, is the path to liberation. You can't change what you don't acknowledge. Right?!

From Vision to Action - The Energetic Shift

Once you have a clearer picture of your exciting financial vision, the next step is to start feeling into it. This isn't about forcing yourself to feel positive; it's about allowing yourself to experience the emotions associated with that future reality. Visualization, meditation, even just quietly contemplating your vision can help. The more you connect with the *feeling* of having that life, the more you start to align your energy with it. And energy, my friend, is everything.

This is where the practical meets the spiritual. You're not just setting goals; you're creating a magnetic field that pulls your desires towards you. You're telling the universe, and more importantly, your subconscious mind, what you're truly available for. And when your subconscious is on board, amazing things start to happen.

Now, this doesn't mean you sit back and wait for money to magically appear. Far from it. It means your actions become infused with purpose. The "boring" tasks of budgeting, saving, or investing suddenly have a deeper meaning. They're not just tasks; they're steps towards that exciting vision. You're not depriving yourself; you're investing in your future self, the self that lives that dream life. You're not just saving for a house; you're saving for the feeling of security and belonging that house represents.

This is also where you start to gently challenge those old money scripts. When a thought comes up like, "I'll never have enough," you can acknowledge it, but then consciously pivot back to your vision. You can ask, "Is that thought serving my exciting future, or holding me back?" You're retraining your brain, bit by bit, to support your desires instead of sabotaging them.

Brené Brown's work, particularly in Rising Strong (paid link), reminds us that vulnerability and courage are essential in this process. It takes courage to dream big, to face your fears, and to step into a new narrative for your life and your money. It's not always easy, but it's always worth it.

Integrating Your Vision into Daily Life

An exciting financial vision isn't something you look at once a year and forget. It's a living, breathing guide. Here's how to keep it alive:

  • Create a Vision Board (Physical or Digital): Fill it with images, words, and symbols that represent your exciting financial future. Look at it daily. Feel the feelings.
  • Schedule "Vision Time": Dedicate 5-10 minutes each day to simply sit with your vision. Visualize it. Journal about it. Let it permeate your being.
  • Review Your Vision Regularly: Once a week, or at least once a month, revisit your written vision. Read it aloud. Does it still land? Has anything shifted? Your vision isn't static; it evolves as you do.
  • Make Decisions Through Your Vision: Before making a financial decision, big or small, ask yourself: "Does this align with my exciting financial vision? Does this move me closer to the life I want to create?" This simple question can be incredibly powerful.
  • Celebrate Small Wins: Every step you take, no matter how small, that moves you towards your vision deserves recognition. Celebrate it! This reinforces positive behavior and keeps your momentum going.

This isn't about being rigid. It's about being intentional. It's about constantly reminding yourself of the "why" behind your financial choices. When you have a compelling "why," the "how" becomes so much easier, and frankly, more enjoyable. Does that land?

Consider the teachings of Tara Brach in Radical Acceptance (paid link). She speaks about accepting our present reality, even the uncomfortable parts, as a pathway to freedom. This applies to your current financial situation too. You don't have to love where you are right now, but accepting it, without judgment, allows you to move forward with clarity and compassion, rather than resistance and self-blame. You can't build an exciting future if you're constantly fighting your present.

This isn't just about money. It's about your life. It's about reclaiming your power. It's about living authentically. Your financial vision is a powerful tool to help you do just that. It's a roadmap to your best life, paved with intention and fueled by inspiration. So stop settling for a boring financial life. You deserve so much more.

FAQ

What if I don't know what excites me?

That's a common feeling, and it's okay. Start with what you know you *don't* want. What makes you feel drained, stressed, or unhappy about money? Sometimes clarifying the "no" helps you uncover the "yes." Also, think about moments in your life when you felt truly alive, joyful, or free - what were you doing? What was happening? Those feelings are clues. Experiment. Try new things. Pay attention to what sparks your curiosity. Your desires aren't always obvious; sometimes you have to dig for them.

Is this just wishful thinking?

Absolutely not. This isn't about wishing; it's about intentionality and alignment. Wishful thinking is passive. Creating an exciting financial vision is an active process that involves deep self-inquiry, emotional engagement, and then, crucial action. It's about directing your energy and focus, and then taking consistent steps in that direction. You can't hit a target you can't see, and you can't be excited about a future you haven't envisioned. This vision is your target, your compass, and your fuel.

How do I deal with skepticism or fear that comes up when I dream big?

Skepticism and fear are natural. They're often protective mechanisms from your subconscious, trying to keep you "safe" in familiar territory, even if that territory is uncomfortable. Don't fight them. Acknowledge them. Say, "Hello, fear. I see you." Then gently remind yourself of your vision. This is where self-compassion is key. You're not trying to eliminate fear; you're learning to move forward *with* it. The more you practice connecting with your vision and taking small, aligned actions, the more your confidence will grow, and the quieter that fear will become. Remember, wealth isn't what you accumulate. It's what you stop fearing.

Your financial vision is waiting for you. It's not out there somewhere, a hidden treasure. It's inside you, waiting to be unearthed. It's a reflection of your deepest desires, your inherent worth, and your potential. It's time to stop letting fear, scarcity, or external expectations dictate your financial future. It's time to claim a vision that truly makes your heart sing. So, what's one small step you can take today to start unearthing that vision?

Om Shanti Shanti Shanti
meta_description: Craft an inspiring financial vision. Learn to move beyond scarcity and create a plan that genuinely excites you for your future.
author: Kalesh
